headline: How Cuba's Solar Tricycles Became the City's New Lifeline
meta_description: Facing fuel scarcity, Havana residents adapted Chinese electric tricycles with solar panels, creating an unexpected transportation revolution.
summary_short: When oil tankers stopped arriving in Cuba, residents didn't wait for rescue. They built their own solution—one solar panel at a time.
body:
When oil shipments to Cuba dropped from eight tankers monthly to just one, the island faced its worst fuel crisis in years. Rolling blackouts spread, bus routes vanished, and Havana's legendary vintage American cars—those mechanical survivors of decades past—suddenly seemed obsolete.

Into the gap came an unlikely answer: Chinese-made electric tricycles, costing between $2,000 and $4,000 each. For a country where state workers earn around $10 monthly, these vehicles should have remained out of reach. Yet Cubans found ways. Some sold old gasoline cars to buy them. Others received them from relatives abroad. Small-business owners invested their profits, betting the tricycles would pay for themselves.

They were right. The vehicles now follow abandoned bus routes, haul produce to markets, and collect garbage in Havana neighborhoods. A ride costs about 500 Cuban pesos—less than a dollar—making them accessible to workers earning between $10 and $40 monthly.

But the real innovation came next. Many owners installed solar panels on awnings over passenger seats, allowing vehicles to recharge while parked or moving. The $500 modification pays off quickly given the grid's unreliability.

As engineer Carlos Álvarez observed, you cannot spend ten minutes on a Havana street without seeing countless tricycles pass by. What began as emergency adaptation has become permanent infrastructure. The city has reorganized around these vehicles—and shows no sign of looking back.
